texts.label: text
texts.orig: Ce travail fait partie d'un projet plus large qui vise à identifier les dimensionnements optimaux pour les procédés de séparation membranaires multi-étagés. Le projet a débuté en 2015 par une collaboration entre le LORIA (Laboratoire Lorrain de Recherche en Informatique et ses Applications de l' Université de Lorraine) et le LRGP (Laboratoire Réactions et Génie des Procédés). L'équipe a identifié des modèles et des algorithmes basés sur des approches de programmation mathématique, visant à développer des outils de conception automatique de procédé de séparation membranaire dédiés dans un pemier temps au traitement des gaz. Ces outils numériques ont pour but de pouvoir intégrer une fonction objectif réaliste et augmenter les degrés de liberté dans la recherche d'architecture de procédés possibles par rapport aux solutions de l'état de l'art actuel.
texts.text: Ce travail fait partie d'un projet plus large qui vise à identifier les dimensionnements optimaux pour les procédés de séparation membranaires multi-étagés. Le projet a débuté en 2015 par une collaboration entre le LORIA (Laboratoire Lorrain de Recherche en Informatique et ses Applications de l' Université de Lorraine) et le LRGP (Laboratoire Réactions et Génie des Procédés). L'équipe a identifié des modèles et des algorithmes basés sur des approches de programmation mathématique, visant à développer des outils de conception automatique de procédé de séparation membranaire dédiés dans un pemier temps au traitement des gaz. Ces outils numériques ont pour but de pouvoir intégrer une fonction objectif réaliste et augmenter les degrés de liberté dans la recherche d'architecture de procédés possibles par rapport aux solutions de l'état de l'art actuel.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Le processus de séparation des gases par membrane est largement étudié en raison en particulier de son utilisation dans la production d'énergie renouvelable. La production d'hydrogène comme vecteur énergétique est considérée d'une extrême importance ces derniers temps. Ma thèse a été développée autour de l'étude de cas des séparations CO 2 H 2 , avec comme objectifs la production d'un H 2 de pureté et taux de récupération les plus élevés possible. En utilisant des membranes de différents matériaux, nous avons d'abord résolu un cas simple de sépration d'un système binaire CO 2 H 2 , puis nous l'avons étendu à trois gaz. Ce problème est modélisé comme un problème MINLP basé sur une superstructure. Plusieurs techniques d'optimisation globale sont développées pour le résoudre. L'approche de l'apprentissage automatique a la capacité de résoudre ces problèmes sans nécessiter un modèle complexe. Nous avons donc développé un réseau neuronal artificiel pour représenter les contraintes non linéaires et non convexes, inhérentes au comportement de la membrane, permettant de remplacer le modèle physique dans le problème mathématique. Il s'agit donc d'un modèle de substitution. Pour entrainer le réseau de neurones, un ensemble de données est nécessaire. Ce travail tente de développer divers algorithmes pour choisir intelligemment cet ensemble de données. Nous avons commencé par utiliser la technique d'échantillonnage, et les méthodes d'augmentation de données. Deux algorithmes sont ici développés : (i) ADORO (Augmentated Dataset driven-by Optimization by Random Oscillation) qui ajoute des points aux données initiales dans les zones d'intérêt pour l'optimisation (ii) AAD (Adversarially Augmentated Dataset) qui met en œuvre une technique classique d'augmentation des données, des points perturbés par la descente du gradient. L'objectif est d'obtenir un modèle de substitution très précis, en réduisant considérablement la taille de l'ensemble de données et en réduisant également le temps de la procédure.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: duction des gaz à effets de serre parce qu'elle se consacre à un processus très utilisé dans l'industrie et extrêmement respectueux de l'environnement : les procédés de séparation des gaz par voie membranaire.
texts.text: duction des gaz à effets de serre parce qu'elle se consacre à un processus très utilisé dans l'industrie et extrêmement respectueux de l'environnement : les procédés de séparation des gaz par voie membranaire.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Différentes technologies de séparation ont été utilisées depuis de nombreuses anénes dans le traitement des effluents : distillation, absorption physique ou chimique, extraction ou encore séchage. Les limites de ces technologies (c'est-à-dire la performance, la compacité, la flexibilité, la consommation d'énergie) ont conduit à la recherche de méthodes alternatives. Cette recherche de différenciation a conduit à l'introduction de procédés de séparation basés sur des membranes. Les technologies membranaires ont pris une importance croissante dans les applications industrielles au cours des dernières décennies. Elles sont devenues un procédé de référence dans l'industrie alimentaire, le traitement de l'eau et le dessalement, et elles prennent une place et un rôle plus importants dans d'autres secteurs tels que les domaines de la santé, la médecine et la pharmacie, l'énergie, le traitement des gaz et l'industrie pétrochimique. Les procédés membranaires se retrouvent également dans d'autres domaines industriels tels que la purification et la récupération de substrats, la récupération et le recyclage de catalyseurs et de solvants, la concentration de solutions, le nettoyage de cristaux, la filtration, la clarification de flux de processus et la purification de flux de déchets, etc. Les unités à membrane ont généralement une empreinte au sol relativement faible, avec dans la plupart des cas, une réduction intéressante de la consommation d'énergie et de l'utilisation de produits chimiques (le traitement des gaz par perméation gazeuse est sans solvant). La simplicité de fonctionnement et d'installation des systèmes à membrane, ainsi que leur modularité, permettent des changements d'échelle relativement aisés et une grande flexibilité. Le fonctionnement en régime permanent avec recyclage partiel ou complet du rétentat/perméat est un autre avantage des systèmes à membrane.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Il existe différents types de membranes (polymères, inorganiques), et chaque matériau se différencie par des perméabilités différentes pour chaque composé gazeux. Le type de procédé membranaire sera ainsi choisi en fonction de l'objectif à atteindre, comme la perméation des gaz, la pervaporation, la distillation par membrane, l'osmose inverse, l'ultrafiltration, la microfiltration, la dialyse et l'électrodialyse, etc.
texts.text: Il existe différents types de membranes (polymères, inorganiques), et chaque matériau se différencie par des perméabilités différentes pour chaque composé gazeux. Le type de procédé membranaire sera ainsi choisi en fonction de l'objectif à atteindre, comme la perméation des gaz, la pervaporation, la distillation par membrane, l'osmose inverse, l'ultrafiltration, la microfiltration, la dialyse et l'électrodialyse, etc.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Pour la séparation de mélange homogène (gaz ou liquide), les processus de transfert sont principalement gouverné par la pression. Quand un mélange de gaz, appelé "Feed", entre dans la membrane, les composants ayant les plus grandes perméabilités passent principalement à travers la membrane formant le perméat obtenu à basse pression, tandis que les composants ayant les perméabilités les plus faibles restent majoritairement au compartiment haute pression, formant le rétentat.
texts.text: Pour la séparation de mélange homogène (gaz ou liquide), les processus de transfert sont principalement gouverné par la pression. Quand un mélange de gaz, appelé "Feed", entre dans la membrane, les composants ayant les plus grandes perméabilités passent principalement à travers la membrane formant le perméat obtenu à basse pression, tandis que les composants ayant les perméabilités les plus faibles restent majoritairement au compartiment haute pression, formant le rétentat.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Dans cette thèse, nous nous réunissons sur deux études de cas ayant toutes deux pour objectif la purification de l'hydrogène. Nous étudierons le cas de la séparation de l'H 2 de CO 2 , puis de la séparation de H 2 de deux autres gaz, le CO 2 et le N 2 , en utilisant dans les deux cas deux types de membranes différents.
texts.text: Dans cette thèse, nous nous réunissons sur deux études de cas ayant toutes deux pour objectif la purification de l'hydrogène. Nous étudierons le cas de la séparation de l'H 2 de CO 2 , puis de la séparation de H 2 de deux autres gaz, le CO 2 et le N 2 , en utilisant dans les deux cas deux types de membranes différents.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: La conception optimale de procédés de séparation des gaz par membrane utilise deux leviers principaux : (a) la recherche de la meilleure conception de procédé et (b) l'utilisation du meilleur matériau membranaire. Aujourd'hui, en raison de l'augmentation des ap-
texts.text: La conception optimale de procédés de séparation des gaz par membrane utilise deux leviers principaux : (a) la recherche de la meilleure conception de procédé et (b) l'utilisation du meilleur matériau membranaire. Aujourd'hui, en raison de l'augmentation des ap-

texts.label: text
texts.orig: plications de la technologie de séparation des gaz par membrane dans l'industrie, le développement d'une stratégie de conception précise, efficace et fiable est essentielle. Pour concevoir un système efficace de séparation des gaz par membrane, différents aspects doivent être étudiés. La plupart des publications récentes sur la séparation des gaz par membrane se sont concentrées sur une série d'approches différentes pour promouvoir la conception d'un système de séparation par membrane (c'est-à-dire le matériau de la membrane, la conception du module de la membrane et la conception des procédés de séparation des gaz par membrane). Outre l'amélioration des matériaux et de la conception des modules membranaires, la conception efficace des procédés de séparation des gaz par membrane revêt une grande importance pour l'industrie.
texts.text: plications de la technologie de séparation des gaz par membrane dans l'industrie, le développement d'une stratégie de conception précise, efficace et fiable est essentielle. Pour concevoir un système efficace de séparation des gaz par membrane, différents aspects doivent être étudiés. La plupart des publications récentes sur la séparation des gaz par membrane se sont concentrées sur une série d'approches différentes pour promouvoir la conception d'un système de séparation par membrane (c'est-à-dire le matériau de la membrane, la conception du module de la membrane et la conception des procédés de séparation des gaz par membrane). Outre l'amélioration des matériaux et de la conception des modules membranaires, la conception efficace des procédés de séparation des gaz par membrane revêt une grande importance pour l'industrie.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Ce travail est focalisé sur la recherche de la conception optimale. Les contraintes généralement imposées pour nos cas d'étude sont l'atteinte de la plus grande pureté et du plus haut taux de récupération d'un composé. Ces deux contraintes sont antagonistes dans tout problème de séparation. Ce problème est traité dans la littérature sous différents angles : simulation, optimisation, ou modèles de substitution de type apprentissage automatique. Il existe plusieurs logiciels de simulation pour la séparation des gaz par membrane. Cependant, la simulation ne permet d'évaluer qu'un nombre restreint de configurations. Dans le cas d'un processus de membrane à plusieurs étapes (où chaque étape représente une membrane), le nombre de configurations à simuler, présenté schématiquement Fig.2 la superstructure, explose et les temps de calcul requis rendent impossible l' eur seule utilisation. Ainsi, pour trouver la meilleure conception en minimisant le coût et le temps, différents algorithmes d'optimisation sont développés. Lorsque le problème mathématique est modélisé, les degrés de liberté pris en compte sont très élevés, c'est pourquoi certaines hypothèses sont faites pour le simplifier. Beaucoup de techniques déjà proposées dans la littérature ont des conditions de fonctionnement limitées.L'obtention de la meilleure configuration est résolue par la programmation linéaire en nombres entiers mélangés (MINLP en anglais, pour "mixed-integer nonlinear programming"). La formulation du résultat de ce problème est compliquée et comporte de nombreuses contraintes non linéaires et non convexes. Ainsi, le temps de calcul pour résoudre le problème et pour trouver la solution optimale est très important. Le problème est d'autant plus difficile à résoudre qu'il y a d'étapes et que la pureté requise du produit est élevée. L'introduction d'hypothèse simplificatrices a en plus l'avantage d'éliminer de nombreuses solutions irréablisables en pratique.

L'introduction d'hypothèse simplificatrices a en plus l'avantage d'éliminer de nombreuses solutions irréablisables en pratique.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: De nos jours, de nombreuses techniques d'apprentissage automatique sont développées pour tenter de résoudre ce problème en introduisant un modèle de substitution. Il s'agit d'une technique utilisée en ingénierie lorsqu'un résultat ne peut être évalué facilement ou directement, et qu'un modèle de substitution doit être utilisé à la place. Les expériences et/ou les simulations sont souvent utilisées pour évaluer la relation entre les objectifs de conception, les fonctions de contrainte et les variables de conception. Dans la première partie de cette thèse, nous montrons la nécessité d'introduire un modèle de Machine Learning. L'objectif est d'approcher le comportement de la membrane, phénomènes physiques, en utilisant un Réseau Neural Artificiel, plutôt que resoudre le systeme complexe d'équations différentielles non linéaires. Pour entraîner le réseau neuronal artificiel,

texts.orig: un ensemble de données est nécessaire. Dans la deuxième partie de cette thèse, nous montrons comment générer ces données de manière efficace en ne considérant des points que dans les zones pertinentes à l'optimization global. Nous introduisons différentes techniques d'augmentation des données pour maximiser la précision du modèle tout en minimisant le temps total de la procédure. Dans la littérature, plusieurs chercheurs proposent une approche basée sur l'échantillonnage. Une première étape consiste à partir d'un petit ensemble de données. Une deuxième intègre de nouveaux points dans la zone d'intérêt. Une troisième définit un ensemble de critères d'arrêt.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Ce travail est organisé comme suit :
texts.text: Ce travail est organisé comme suit :

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Le chapitre I illustre brièvement le processus de séparation des gaz par membrane. Nous expliquerons les différents matériaux membranaires, la conception des membranes,et le mécanisme de transfert de matière dans une membrane. Nous introduisons le concept de perméabilité et la configuration de l'écoulement. Nous portons une attention particulière aux variables qui caractérisent le modèle d'apprentissage automatique. Le modèle mathématique utilisé comme base de référence est présenté ainsi que toutes les hypothèses simplificatrices qui y sont faites. L'algorithme d'optimisation globale que nous utilisons pour résoudre le problème est présenté et discuté. L'étape fondamentale de validation des solutions est réalisée grâce à un simulateur "maison" MEMSIC. La fin du chapitre évoque les inconvénients que présente cette technique, et motive l'introduction de l'apprentissage automatique.
texts.text: Le chapitre I illustre brièvement le processus de séparation des gaz par membrane. Nous expliquerons les différents matériaux membranaires, la conception des membranes,et le mécanisme de transfert de matière dans une membrane. Nous introduisons le concept de perméabilité et la configuration de l'écoulement. Nous portons une attention particulière aux variables qui caractérisent le modèle d'apprentissage automatique. Le modèle mathématique utilisé comme base de référence est présenté ainsi que toutes les hypothèses simplificatrices qui y sont faites. L'algorithme d'optimisation globale que nous utilisons pour résoudre le problème est présenté et discuté. L'étape fondamentale de validation des solutions est réalisée grâce à un simulateur "maison" MEMSIC. La fin du chapitre évoque les inconvénients que présente cette technique, et motive l'introduction de l'apprentissage automatique.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Le chapitre II présente les contraintes de l'apprentissage automatique et la façon dont nous les intégrons dans le problème d'optimisation. La technique de génération de jeux de données pour l'entraînement du réseaux de neurones est présentée et expliquée en détail. La phase d'échantillonnage est également décrite. Le simulateur est présenté, et ses problèmes de convergence sont mis en évidence. Nous expliquons comment le résoudre grâce à la Machine à Vecteur de Support (SVM). Nous montrerons le choix des meilleurs hyperparamètres pour le réseaux de neurones.Les différentes méthodes présentées sont comparées.
texts.text: Le chapitre II présente les contraintes de l'apprentissage automatique et la façon dont nous les intégrons dans le problème d'optimisation. La technique de génération de jeux de données pour l'entraînement du réseaux de neurones est présentée et expliquée en détail. La phase d'échantillonnage est également décrite. Le simulateur est présenté, et ses problèmes de convergence sont mis en évidence. Nous expliquons comment le résoudre grâce à la Machine à Vecteur de Support (SVM). Nous montrerons le choix des meilleurs hyperparamètres pour le réseaux de neurones.Les différentes méthodes présentées sont comparées.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Le chapitre III présente les deux algorithmes d'augmentation de données développés. Le principe général est de commencer à partir d'un petit nombre de points, et d'en ajouter uniquement dans les zones qui nous intéressent. Ces algorithmes sont développés dans le but de réduire les temps de calcul et d'augmenter la précision du modèle d'apprentissage automatique. Une comparaison entre les deux algorithmes d'augmentation des données est présentée. Tous deux procèdent en perturbant les points du jeu de données initial pour en ajouter de nouveaux dans les zones d'intérêt. Nous proposons un jeu de données augmenté par optimisation par oscillation aléatoire (ADORO) qui ajoute des points en suivant le solution stationnaire trouvée par l'optimisation globale. Dans le cas de jeu de données augmenté de manière adversariale (ADA) les points sont perturbés par descente de gradient.

Dans le cas de jeu de données augmenté de manière adversariale (ADA) les points sont perturbés par descente de gradient.

texts.orig: Membrane separation processes have been widely used in industry during the last decades. Their fields of application are very broad and mostly address liquid and gas treatments. Their main advantages Lin et al. (2015a) are compacity thanks to very high specific transfer area (thousands of m 2 /m 3 ) , reduced footprint, modularity and flexibility, easy scale-up with applicability of the concept of numbering-up instead of more complex scale-up methodologies for competitive processes (packed column, thermal processes), solvent-free processes, low energy requirements mostly dedicated to pressure or vacuum devices. Desalination, water purification, medicine, and health care are of the most known applications with membrane processes based on reverse osmosis, ultrafiltration, microfiltration or dialysis technologies. More recently, gas treatment field offers new and highly challenging opportunities to the academic and industrial membrane community. Among the numerous potential applications of membrane gas separation, some of them are of very high interests Baker (2012):

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The main challenges addressed to membrane processes (for both liquid and gas phases) are dual :
texts.text: The main challenges addressed to membrane processes (for both liquid and gas phases) are dual :

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. The first challenge concern material science. The main target here is to identify the best material (type and shape) able to reach the targeted main separation performances (usually purity and recovery of identified components). This research community is very dynamic and productive and numerous material from different types, are produced and studied : polymeric (elastomeric or glassy) materials for gas treatment, inorganic material (zeolite, Palladium, ceramic, carbon materials), mixed matrix membranes, developed in flat or hollow fibers configurations. Industrialization of those numerous materials from laboratories to commercial modules is still challenging. For gas treatment, just a few commercial references are available often dedicated to air separation, H 2 purification and CO 2 capture

Depending on the expected performance indicators of purity and recovery, different configurations and associated operating conditions are possible. High targeted purities and recovery rates generally demand multi-stage complex processes, with high pressure

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The main target of this process will be to achieve a certain level of purity and recovery rate. The objective function to be minimized can be of many different kinds. The classical expectations are to find the best compromise between the size of the operation units used in the process (related to CAPEX) and the required energy (related to the OPEX). Those functions are generally classically antagonist. Cost functions will be used in this work to quantify this compromise by integrating the costs of membrane area and frame, compressors, vacuum pumps, product losses and some operational expenditure including maintenance cost. Classical physical laws will be applied: flow conservation, material balance, component balance, superstructure flow conservation, performance, and variable domains bounded by realistic values (maximum recycling ratio, pressure ratios, etc.). This problem is reported in the literature from different points of view: simulation, optimization, or surrogate Machine Learning models related to simulation, in our case Artificial Neural Network (ANN).

texts.text: Simulation-based softwares for membrane gas separation already commercially exist : ASPEN, PROSIM, ProII, etc. They allow engineers to simulate and predict the performances of any process (separation, reaction, energy transformation) as soon as operation units simulation toolboxes are available (it is surprisingly not always the case for mem-

texts.label: text
texts.orig: brane) and the entire process configuration is known. Parametric analysis and optimization tools are generally available that could minimize an objective function by varying the operating conditions for a known process configuration. Searching for the optimal design of membrane gas separation (process architecture and associated operating conditions) using the simulation is difficult and uncertain. As shown in the superstructure Fig. 2, trying all the possible configurations in the case of a multistage membrane process (each stage represented a single membrane) is an error-prone and highly time consuming process, the number of combinations increases drastically with multistage process.

texts.orig: Different optimization approaches were proposed in the literature to address this problem. Some of the existing works use different simplified assumptions in the model formulation. Indeed, the number of possible configurations increased exponentially with the degree of freedom of the problem. Obviously to optimize the performance and the global cost of the process we had to develop some dedicated design strategy. A Global Optimization algorithm ad hoc and a mathematical model to find the optimal solution was developed for this membrane gas separation problem by Ramírez-Santos et al. (2018). Our work uses the optimization and modeling approach presented in Bozorg (2019) as a baseline, and improve upon it, by the introduction of machine learning approaches. The design

The formulation of this problem is complicated with a lot of non-linear and non-convex constraints, as membrane behavior equation. So the computational time to solve the problem and to find the optimal solution is expensive; of course more stages and more purity and recovery we will ask and more the problem will be difficult to solve. Nowadays a lot of Machine Learning techniques are developed to try to solve this problem by introducing a surrogate model. It is a technique used in engineering when an outcome of interest cannot be readily and directly evaluated, and a model of the outcome is used instead. texts.orig: The behavior of the membrane is represented by discretizing the membrane, with each cell referencing a non-linear and non-convex constraint that is the mathematical transcript of the differential membranes equations. This problem is really difficult to be solved, time consuming and not so accurate when high purity on the product is expected. To simplify this model, we have introduced a surrogate model, which aims to replace the system of non-linear and non-convex equations with a single non-linear equation that is obtained through optimizing an ANN, this concept is shown Fig. 3.

texts.orig: Using the surrogate model helps the mathematical model by reducing the number of non-linear and non-convex constraints added. A dataset generation step has been added via the simulator. We introduce some data augmentation techniques, as shown in Papernot et al. (2016), to be as precise as possible by minimizing the total time of the procedure. In literature several researchers propose a sampling-based approach starting from small dataset, new points are integrated into the area of interest, and some stop criteria is defined. Based on this we develop an ad hoc method for our membrane separation problem. In Fig. 4, we report the goals that we want to achieve in this thesis, by developing data augmentation techniques.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Chapter I briefly illustrates the membrane gas separation process. We will explain the different membrane materials, membrane design, and the mass transfer mechanism in a membrane. The concept of the permeability and the flow configuration are introduced. We will pay particular attention to the variables that characterize the Machine Learning model and we will show also the driven equipment used by it. We will explain the mathematical model used as a baseline and all the simplifying assumptions that have been made. The GO algorithm that we use to solve the problem is shown. Some disadvantages of the discretization techniques are presented.

The concept of the permeability and the flow configuration are introduced. We will pay particular attention to the variables that characterize the Machine Learning model and we will show also the driven equipment used by it. We will explain the mathematical model used as a baseline and all the simplifying assumptions that have been made. The GO algorithm that we use to solve the problem is shown. Some disadvantages of the discretization techniques are presented.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Chapter II introduces the Machine Learning constraints and the way they have been implemented in the Optimization problem. We will explain in detail the dataset generation technique for the ANN train. The sampling phases are reported also with the explanation of the simulator and the problem of convergence. We will show the choice of the best hyperparameters for the ANN. And, at the end, we will add some results for the comparison of the different method.

texts.orig: Chapter IV reports a comparison of two different case-studies based on H 2 purification from H 2 / CO 2 mixtures. Performances of the proposed methodology and ADORO algorithm, but also some issues that open new perspectives, will be illustrated through two levels of complexity : a binary H 2 / CO 2 system and a ternary H 2 / CO 2 / N 2 one.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The membrane gas separation principles and models are presented in this chapter, principally through the description of the physical phenomena that occur. The operating parameters and equipment have a crucial role in the gas separation and are required to define any membrane process design. The membrane mathematical model used by the MEMSIC simulator Bounaceur et al. (2017) is shown. We briefly explain the two different approaches to solve this problem, the cell model is presented and the global optimization used. Two case-studies will be briefly explained: H 2 / CO 2 and H 2 / CO 2 / N 2 .

texts.orig: It is only in the last decades that the membrane gas separation process finds application on an industrial scale, as explained in Bernardo and Clarizia (2013), where an excursus about the utilization of the membrane gas separation technique is proposed. Their main advantages, as already presented, are solvent free processes, smaller footprint and generally lower energy requirement. This technology has grown a lot over the years, and this growth has led to membranes becoming competitive on the market Sridhar et al. (2014); Caballero et al. (2009); Graham (1866); Baker (2001). The first major industrial application of this technique was implemented by Amphlett et al. (1996) for the separation of hydrogen from the purge flow of ammonia plants. The membrane technology has a lot of popularity because on one side it can be used for different processes like nanofiltration, ultrafiltration, gas separation, vapor permeation, pervaporation, microfiltration, reverse osmosis, dialysis, and electrolysis, and on the other side has a lot of advantages like no regeneration step is needed, it is modular so it is easy to install and to scale up, it is flexible and it uses less energy than classical processes Henis (2018). This technique received a lot of interest from the chemical companies, industrial gas suppliers, oil producers, and refineries as described in Bernardo and Drioli (2010). Several applications have achieved commercial success: nitrogen production from the air, hydrogen removal from ammonia scrubbing gas, carbon dioxide removal from natural gas, and enhanced oil recovery. The first industrial hydrogen separation plants in the early 1980s significantly improved the quality of the membranes for separating air and CO 2 . During these years the membrane gas separation process was largely improved in terms of configurations and applications. This process was fast thanks to the progress in material science and membrane design. In material science, thousand of different materials have been developed but only some

texts.orig: In membrane design different optimization and ML techniques are developed. To design a membrane separation process different equipments are chosen, as membrane modules, compressor, vacuum pump, etc. Considering the entire process architecture, several separation stages (membranes) may be necessary to increase the requested purity, recycling between stages (from permeate or retentate sides) can be useful and necessary to increase recovery rate. Multiple process configurations are possible for such a multistage membrane separation, i.e. number of membrane stages, type of membrane material per stage, membrane surface of each stage, upstream and downstream pressure of each stage and stream's connections between stages including partial or total recycling, as reported in Zarca et al. (2018).

texts.orig: The idea of the membrane gas separation is that when a mixture of gas enters a membrane module [FEED], its components will be separated into the permeate and the retentate side, among their relative ability to permeate the membrane material as shown in Fig. 1.1. The mass transfer of each component is driven by the partial pressure difference between both compartment, the high pressure from the FEED and the retentate side, and the low-pressure side on the permeate side.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: One of the characteristic of the membrane process is the used material, which will define a selective permeation capacity for each gas. Different types of materials can be used for the membrane gas separation. The choice of the best material to use for the separation of a certain gas is defined thanks to some features of the components of gases to be separated. Different categories of membrane exist: polymeric, inorganic and mixed matrix. The most used membranes for gas separation in industry are dense polymeric material for their broad separation properties and relative low production cost. We report different examples of polymeric membranes like Cellulose acetate (CA), polyacrylonitrile (PAN), polyimide, polycarbonate (PC), polyethylene (PE), polypropylene (PP), and polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE). Those membranes exhibit for most of them interesting chemical resistance, sorption, diffusion capacity, and stability at moderate temperatures. The development of these polymeric membranes and the use of them are gaining momentum, especially through new challenges and increasing demands from the industry Budd and McKeown (2010); Yampolskii (2012).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The permeability is main characteristic of the membrane that allows to quantify the transfer of any permeable gas component through the material under the driving force expressed as the difference in partial pressure between both interfaces of the membrane. Permeability represents the units of component rate per surface, per thickness, and per unit of pressure difference. Selectivity represents the capability of a membrane material to allow the separation of two components (Permeability has to be different) Park et al. (2017). The ideal selectivity (between two components) is expressed as the ratio of the permeabilities. Another metric which describes the ability of the components to percolate through the membrane is the Permeance. The permeance is the ratio of the permeability and the material thickness and allows to experimentally characterize membrane without the tricky determination of the thickness.

texts.orig: The diffusivity and solubility are known to be negatively correlated. The first studies on the topic are the works of Robeson (1991) and Robeson (2008) that study the relationship between Selectivity and Permeability. It appears that highly permeable membranes are less selective. Robeson clearly exhibits the existing trade-off between permeability and selectivity for polymeric membranes, as shown in Fig. 1.3. The upper bound, considered as the empirical limit, is shown that represents the maximum achievable selectivity for a given permeability at a current time.

texts.orig: The main objective is to achieve some purity and recovery on this targeted product, by minimizing the cost of the process. The problem become even more complicated whenever we desire to achieve both high purity and recovery. In this case a multistage process is generally needed. Multiple solutions and design exist that can be considered with: multistage membrane separation, several stages, and optimized membrane surface and pressure ratios of each stage , as shown in Saif et al. (2009). The superstructure representing all the possible combinations for a 2 stage membrane process is presented in Fig. 1.5.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As already explained finding the optimum design of membrane gas separation using simulation is difficult, uncertain and time consuming. That is why various optimization approaches have been proposed in the literature. From the mathematical modeling point of view several authors propose Mixed Integer Non Linear Programming approaches (MINLP) Qi and Henson (1998a), and some others Non Linear Programming (NLP) formulation Bozorg et al. (2019). Depending on the application different numbers of stage are considered. Most reported results from the literature considered between 1 and 3 stages Uppaluri et al. (2004); Qi and Henson (1998a); Scholz et al. (2015); Ohs et al. (2016); Gabrielli et al. (2017), more rarely 4 stage processes are considered Qi and Henson (2000); Arias et al. (2016); Qi and Henson (1998b).

texts.orig: For generating data and for the validation of our result, the MEMSIC simulator is used, MEMSIC is a CAPE-OPEN module that simulates gas permeation membrane module Bounaceur et al. (2017). In conjunction with process simulation software (such as Aspen, Hysys, Pro/II, and PROSIM), it allows the simulation of multistage separation. MEMSIC is extremely precise and reliable. For simulating multistage systems it requires the use of a graphical interface, where input parameters can be implemented, as shown in Fig. 1.9.

texts.orig: The objective function is to minimize the total annual separation cost. It is composed of both capital and operational expenditure. Capital expenditure (CAPEX) includes membrane area and frame, compressors, and vacuum pumps. The operational expenditure (OPEX) includes electricity related to compressor and vacuum equipment, membrane replacement, operation, and maintenance cost.It is worth noting that the amount of flow passing through devices such as compressors, vacuum pumps, and membranes can affect both the CAPEX and OPEX associated with these devices.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1.4.1. H 2 / CO 2 separation for H 2 purification
texts.text: 1.4.1. H 2 / CO 2 separation for H 2 purification

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In recent years, there has been a move to turn energy production toward renewable sources. A particularly prominent place is increasingly being taken by hydrogen, as shown in Fig. 1.11. At the Paris Congress in 2005, countless funds were allocated by the European Union for a new deposition technique for the development of thin-blended conductive membranes for the separation of hydrogen and oxygen. A modelling study concerning the integration of the developed membranes into hydrogen and fuel systems supported the experimental work. The study provided input to intensify the deposition process and evaluate costs in selected plant configurations to achieve zero CO 2 emissions and minimize CO 2 capture costs. Hydrogen is used as a clean and efficient renewable energy, as its use would lead to a more sustainable energy future. The use of membranes today represents one of the most promising solutions for pre-combustion applications (CO 2 /H 2 separation from syngas). The activity aims to develop and characterize different types of polymeric membranes for separating CO 2 from hydrogen (pre-combustion applications) and for biogas purification. In pre-combustion capture, CO 2 is separated from the syngas before combustion takes place: after gasification and catalytic conversion of carbon monoxide to carbon dioxide (so-called water-gas shift conversion), the syngas consists mainly of CO 2 and H 2 . The separation of gases via membranes also has 'drawbacks' related to the difficulty of obtaining a high-purity product, the need for large working surfaces, and, not least, the need to achieve a compromise between selectivity and permeability. This case-study has an important relevance since hydrogen is considered as a central element in the current debate on the search for alternatives to fossil fuels and non-polluting energy sources, being the most abundant element in the universe. It represents the 'energy vector' and, as such, in recent years, the possibility of using it as an environmentally neutral fuel, particularly in fuel cells, to convert chemical energy into electricity is gaining ground.

The use of membranes today represents one of the most promising solutions for pre-combustion applications (CO 2 /H 2 separation from syngas). The activity aims to develop and characterize different types of polymeric membranes for separating CO 2 from hydrogen (pre-combustion applications) and for biogas purification. In pre-combustion capture, CO 2 is separated from the syngas before combustion takes place: after gasification and catalytic conversion of carbon monoxide to carbon dioxide (so-called water-gas shift conversion), the syngas consists mainly of CO 2 and H 2 . The separation of gases via membranes also has 'drawbacks' related to the difficulty of obtaining a high-purity product, the need for large working surfaces, and, not least, the need to achieve a compromise between selectivity and permeability. This case-study has an important relevance since hydrogen is considered as a central element in the current debate on the search for alternatives to fossil fuels and non-polluting energy sources, being the most abundant element in the universe. It represents the 'energy vector' and, as such, in recent years, the possibility of using it as an environmentally neutral fuel, particularly in fuel cells, to convert chemical energy into electricity is gaining ground.

We will explain how we integrated the ML model into the mathematical programming model and discuss the validation process we used to verify its accuracy through optimization.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the literature the surrogate model is not always used for the same aim and in the same manner. McBride1 and Sundmacher (2018) present a review on the different surrogate models that have used in chemical process engineering. They report on different surrogate models such as kriging, ANN, polynomial, Radial Basis Functions. Furthermore they illustrate the different areas in which such models are feasibility analysis, surrogates for dynamic processes, and surrogate models for optimization.
texts.text: In the literature the surrogate model is not always used for the same aim and in the same manner. McBride1 and Sundmacher (2018) present a review on the different surrogate models that have used in chemical process engineering. They report on different surrogate models such as kriging, ANN, polynomial, Radial Basis Functions. Furthermore they illustrate the different areas in which such models are feasibility analysis, surrogates for dynamic processes, and surrogate models for optimization.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In Wang et al. (2006) a ANN is used to predict the behavior of a membrane in separation processes. The case-study chosen is the hydrogen recovery. The model employed is a radial basis function (RBF) neural network, able to estimate permeate hydrogen concentration, permeate gas flux and residue hydrogen concentration, using as input data operating temperature, feed-side pressure, permeate-side pressure, residue-side pressure, feed-gas flux and feed-hydrogen concentration. The ML model is able to predict the performance parameters of the membrane separation process only on the assumption that the boundaries are omitted.
texts.text: In Wang et al. (2006) a ANN is used to predict the behavior of a membrane in separation processes. The case-study chosen is the hydrogen recovery. The model employed is a radial basis function (RBF) neural network, able to estimate permeate hydrogen concentration, permeate gas flux and residue hydrogen concentration, using as input data operating temperature, feed-side pressure, permeate-side pressure, residue-side pressure, feed-gas flux and feed-hydrogen concentration. The ML model is able to predict the performance parameters of the membrane separation process only on the assumption that the boundaries are omitted.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Carlos A. Henao (2011) developed a superstructure based strategy where complex units (like Reactors, distillation column etc.) are approximated by a surrogate model. They generated the training dataset using a commercial simulator, ASPEN. The problem is formulated like a MINLP. The optimization problem is solved by using General Algebraic Modeling System (GAMS). And thanks the simulator, in their case ASPEN, the dependent variables are defined. The ANN is used to represent the complexity of the problem and can easily be introduced into the mathematical model. They applied the proposal methodology on few simple processes.
texts.text: Carlos A. Henao (2011) developed a superstructure based strategy where complex units (like Reactors, distillation column etc.) are approximated by a surrogate model. They generated the training dataset using a commercial simulator, ASPEN. The problem is formulated like a MINLP. The optimization problem is solved by using General Algebraic Modeling System (GAMS). And thanks the simulator, in their case ASPEN, the dependent variables are defined. The ANN is used to represent the complexity of the problem and can easily be introduced into the mathematical model. They applied the proposal methodology on few simple processes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Soleimani et al. (2013) applied the ML model to predict the flux and the resistance for the treatment of oily wastewaters with commercial polyacrylonitrile membranes. They used two distinct ANN to predict the permeation flux and the other one fouling resistance. The goal is to optimize the operating conditions in separation of oil from industrial oily wastewaters thanks to a genetic algorithm. This is a multi-objective optimization problem with the aims to obtain the optimum operating parameters for a desired permeation flux (i.e. maximum flux) and fouling resistance (i.e. minimum fouling) behaviour and has been conducted by means of the NSGA-II algorithm.

They used two distinct ANN to predict the permeation flux and the other one fouling resistance. The goal is to optimize the operating conditions in separation of oil from industrial oily wastewaters thanks to a genetic algorithm. This is a multi-objective optimization problem with the aims to obtain the optimum operating parameters for a desired permeation flux (i.e. maximum flux) and fouling resistance (i.e. minimum fouling) behaviour and has been conducted by means of the NSGA-II algorithm.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Khayet and Cojocaru (2013) developed a surrogate model for the desalination of the water. The algorithm used to train the model is Levenberg- Marquardt back-propagation. To train the ANN they used an ensemble of 53 experimental data, divided in train validation and test set. Monte Carlo simulation is employed for the optimization.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Ibrahim et al. (2018) used one ANN for each consider output of the crude oil distillation. They don't want to design of heat-integrated crude oil distillation unit that combines a surrogate column model. If the simulator converge they proceed to build the SVM, they will delete this points from the dataset. The SVM is considered as a feasibility classifier for the feasible region of the optimization framework, that applies a genetic algorithm.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The entire dataset is used to train the SVM. The ANN predictions are shown to be in good agreement with the results from the rigorous column model, and the SVM is shown to have some ability to remove unfeasible design options from the search space.
texts.text: The entire dataset is used to train the SVM. The ANN predictions are shown to be in good agreement with the results from the rigorous column model, and the SVM is shown to have some ability to remove unfeasible design options from the search space.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Rall et al. (2019) studied how to link the mass transport properties of LbL-nanofiltration membranes to synthesize membrane fabrication conditions. The ANN is used for two goals: (i) to predict transport, optimize membrane fabrication conditions and upper bound for the rejection and permeation trade-off and (ii) to predict the membrane properties architectures due on the synthetic conditions. This problem is a multi-objective optimization of retention and permeability. The authors developed a deterministic GO and they reformulated this problem to a set of single-objective optimization problems using the glyph[epsilon1] -constraint method. To generate the training dataset, the dynamic coating method develop by Menne et al. (2016) is used.
texts.text: Rall et al. (2019) studied how to link the mass transport properties of LbL-nanofiltration membranes to synthesize membrane fabrication conditions. The ANN is used for two goals: (i) to predict transport, optimize membrane fabrication conditions and upper bound for the rejection and permeation trade-off and (ii) to predict the membrane properties architectures due on the synthetic conditions. This problem is a multi-objective optimization of retention and permeability. The authors developed a deterministic GO and they reformulated this problem to a set of single-objective optimization problems using the glyph[epsilon1] -constraint method. To generate the training dataset, the dynamic coating method develop by Menne et al. (2016) is used.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Rall et al. (2020a) studied an optimization strategy to design the performance nanofiltration membrane modules. They obtain results cheaper respect what the Literature are proposing by using an ANN, they used a combination between the ANN's developed in the work Rall et al. (2019) and an hybrid data-driven model. The ANN is used to compute the salt retention Rj and the permeability Q of a nanofiltration unit, these two variables are used to found the solutions of the main equations.The optimization problem is a multi-objective problem. One of the assumption that they did is to consider a single stage without recirculation. To optimize the operational variables and the membrane selection variables they used deterministic global optimization solver MAiNGO Bongartz et al. (2018). The dataset used to train the ANN is adapted to the mixed data types. In the input of the data we will have a part of variables continuously and integer.

They obtain results cheaper respect what the Literature are proposing by using an ANN, they used a combination between the ANN's developed in the work Rall et al. (2019) and an hybrid data-driven model. The ANN is used to compute the salt retention Rj and the permeability Q of a nanofiltration unit, these two variables are used to found the solutions of the main equations.The optimization problem is a multi-objective problem. One of the assumption that they did is to consider a single stage without recirculation. To optimize the operational variables and the membrane selection variables they used deterministic global optimization solver MAiNGO Bongartz et al. (2018). The dataset used to train the ANN is adapted to the mixed data types. In the input of the data we will have a part of variables continuously and integer.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Rall et al. (2020b) the ANN was trained to learn the ionic separation performance of the membrane, creating a data-driven ANN-based surrogate model. The ANN are trained based on data generated by a one-dimensional extended Nernst-Planck ion transport model and extended to a more accurate two-dimensional distribution of the membrane module, that captures the filtration-related decreasing retention of salt. The surrogate model is used to generate additional data that is trained in a new ANN surrogate model. This model is embedded in a membrane process plant model and the optimal process design is solved by deterministic global process optimization, avoiding the utilization of inaccurate approximations through heuristics or short-cut models.

The ANN are trained based on data generated by a one-dimensional extended Nernst-Planck ion transport model and extended to a more accurate two-dimensional distribution of the membrane module, that captures the filtration-related decreasing retention of salt. The surrogate model is used to generate additional data that is trained in a new ANN surrogate model. This model is embedded in a membrane process plant model and the optimal process design is solved by deterministic global process optimization, avoiding the utilization of inaccurate approximations through heuristics or short-cut models.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Hu et al. (2021) proposed predictive methodologies that will fast-track the industrial implementation of organic solvent nanofiltration (OSN) membranes. The problem in this is a little bit more complicated respect the membrane gas separation because they had to consider all the interaction and the possible connection between solvent-membrane, solute-solvent, and solute-membrane. They proceed to generating a large dataset for training the ANN. Furthermore they use the principal component analysis (PCA) technique to choose the best parameters to train the network. They used three different artificial intelligence (AI) models (artificial neural network, support vector machine, random forest) to predict the membrane performance.

texts.orig: In the Section 1.3.1 we described the mathematical programming model, that we refer as cell-model, that we use as starting point/reference model for our work. The cell model has some inconvenients: the convergence of the local search is time-consuming and for many starting points the solver cannot find any feasible solution. These problems increase when the demand in purity and/or recovery are high. These observations pushed us towards ML. We use the ANN to represent the behavior of a single membrane in Fig. 2.2 so the main equation of the membrane Eq. 1.31, and the other constraints that refers to the cell Eq. 1.24-1.30, will be substitute with the new constraints representing of the ANN predictor.

texts.orig: To train the ANN we need to generate a dataset to span all the feasible region. We want to show that the chosen architecture allows to accurately represent the membrane behavior. As mentioned before for generating the dataset we used the commercial simulator MEMSIC Bounaceur et al. (2017). The simulator is written in FORTRAN. It takes an input vector (that depicts the feasible region in the most optimal manner), and evaluated the output variables by solving the system of Eq. 1.10. Our model has three inputs, and we need model accuracy in the entire input space since we cannot know in advance what are the operating conditions of each membrane in the optimal system design. Drawing inspiration from the literature Bhosekar and Ierapetritou (2018) and Eason and Cremaschi (2014), we have taken into account three distinct methods of generating datasets:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As can be seen from the Fig. 2.6, the points where the simulator does not converge are concentrated in a single region. Therefore we decided to use a support vector machine (SVM) model to remove them from the dataset. For safety, we removed all the points are above the separation hyperplane.
texts.text: As can be seen from the Fig. 2.6, the points where the simulator does not converge are concentrated in a single region. Therefore we decided to use a support vector machine (SVM) model to remove them from the dataset. For safety, we removed all the points are above the separation hyperplane.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: No matter the technique used to generate the dataset, there are input points where the simulator cannot converge, particularly at the boundary values of the input and output variables. For example, there exist input concentration and pressure ratios where it is not possible to obtain a given stage-cut. Fig. 2.6 illustrates in dark red the points in the 2-dimensional grid of φ and θ where the simulator fails for input gas concentration x in 1 = 0 . 049 and x in 1 = 0 . 6 .
texts.text: No matter the technique used to generate the dataset, there are input points where the simulator cannot converge, particularly at the boundary values of the input and output variables. For example, there exist input concentration and pressure ratios where it is not possible to obtain a given stage-cut. Fig. 2.6 illustrates in dark red the points in the 2-dimensional grid of φ and θ where the simulator fails for input gas concentration x in 1 = 0 . 049 and x in 1 = 0 . 6 .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: These points must be removed from the dataset. To identify the problematic area, we built a binary classification problem, where we assign label equal to 1 to all the grid points where the simulation fails, and zero to the others. Then we build a separation hyperplane by training a linear support vector machine (SVM) Piccialli and Sciandrone (2022) for separating the two classes, getting an accuracy equal to 99 . 9% on the grid dataset. We want to not only avoid the points where the simulator will not converge, but also their neighborhood where the simulation may be not reliable due to precision

Then we build a separation hyperplane by training a linear support vector machine (SVM) Piccialli and Sciandrone (2022) for separating the two classes, getting an accuracy equal to 99 . 9% on the grid dataset. texts.orig: In this Section we explain how we represent the behavior of the membrane by using a shallow multi-layer perceptron (MLP) network. In our preliminary results we varied different ANN parameters, before finding the ideal model. We capture the nonlinear input-output relation using a shallow, multi-layer perceptron network, with one layer of neurons and sigmoid activation. This simple network has useful theoretical approximation properties Pinkus (1996). Additionally, because the trained network is then inserted into an optimization problem as equality constraints, the single layer keeps these constraints simple and limits the nonlinearities. Thanks to the simple Fig. 2.7 architecture, the only hyperparameters are the number of neurons in the hidden layer, and the batch size.
texts.text: In this Section we explain how we represent the behavior of the membrane by using a shallow multi-layer perceptron (MLP) network. In our preliminary results we varied different ANN parameters, before finding the ideal model. We capture the nonlinear input-output relation using a shallow, multi-layer perceptron network, with one layer of neurons and sigmoid activation. This simple network has useful theoretical approximation properties Pinkus (1996). Additionally, because the trained network is then inserted into an optimization problem as equality constraints, the single layer keeps these constraints simple and limits the nonlinearities. Thanks to the simple Fig. 2.7 architecture, the only hyperparameters are the number of neurons in the hidden layer, and the batch size.

texts.orig: We use a back-propagation process, mini-batch. The optimizer chosen is Adam, a stochastic gradient descent algorithm.Ruder (2016) showed that this algorithm has a better performances that a lot of algorithms in different tasks. Adam's Keras implementation does not ask to specify the training learning rate, as it is generated and updated according to the optimizer algorithm. The update is based on the mean of the first moments (the mean) as in RMSProp but, unlike the latter, Adam also uses the mean of the second moments of the gradients (the variance not centered).

texts.orig: 1 At the beginning of this work, to make sure we covered the entire search space, we started with the generation of 1 million points. The time for this process was very long, we are on the order of weeks. Looking at the literature, we then wondered whether we would not be able to achieve the same results in terms of purity with a smaller dataset. So we sample for the Grid-search 70 points, to obtain a dataset of 343k points. And we proceed to generate the same amount of points for the Latin Hypercube and the Random Search. Where times are more than halved and purity accuracy is obtained as with the same 1mln point dataset.

texts.orig: For each dataset, we perform a 5-fold validation grid search on the following values of the hyperparameters batch size (BS) and number of neurons (N): BS ∈ [256 , 512] and N ∈ [32 , 64 , 128 , 256 , 512] . We use as loss the mean square error (MSE), and the results on the average MSE were comparable on all the three datasets. For all the datasets, the best configuration was with BS = 256 and N = 64 with similar values of MSE. To get a better measure of the model quality, we look into the absolute error distribution of the output variables on the training set, since we aim to perform well on the whole input space. Fig. 2.8 shows that the hyper dataset performed best, having the lowest absolute error quartiles on the output variables. This is the most important error measure for the case study because we need high precision on the concentration x 1 and the area st for every feasible input. We can not know in advance the operating conditions of each stage that allow to reach the required performance in the multistage system. Therefore, to test the behavior of the model in the interesting areas, we need to evaluate the accuracy when embedded in the optimization model with more than one stage.

texts.orig: It is known that the differential equations 1.3 are numerically difficult for high x 1 purity Neveux et al. (2022), so, we want to verify that the neural network has a good accuracy for this case. Our goal is producing an output satisfying some purity and recovery constraints with a multistage system of CO 2 selective membranes. We consider three levels ( 90% , 95% , 99% ) of purity with recovery as high as possible for a two stage system. The requested recovery is 99% for all purity cases, with the exception of the 99% purity. Indeed, when a 99% purity is requested, a two-stage system allows a recovery of at most 95% .

texts.orig: The ML model is extremely accurate for even the highest purities and recoveries. For very large datasets, the generation, cross and train times are extremely high. However, considering the extraordinary results in terms of model accuracy, we wondered whether it would be possible to develop a data augmentation model. That is, starting with a small set of points and gradually adding them in the areas of greatest interest. So in the following section we proceed to show (i) baselines i.e. by implementing the technique of generating points of different sizes; (ii) adding points by following the gradient descent; (iii) adding points using the GO algorithm, Fig. 3.1.
texts.text: The ML model is extremely accurate for even the highest purities and recoveries. For very large datasets, the generation, cross and train times are extremely high. However, considering the extraordinary results in terms of model accuracy, we wondered whether it would be possible to develop a data augmentation model. That is, starting with a small set of points and gradually adding them in the areas of greatest interest. So in the following section we proceed to show (i) baselines i.e. by implementing the technique of generating points of different sizes; (ii) adding points by following the gradient descent; (iii) adding points using the GO algorithm, Fig. 3.1.

texts.orig: This work develops and compares data augmentation techniques to trade-off between the desired characteristics: solution accuracy, computational time Devabhaktuni and Zhang (2000), and dataset dimension. Data augmentation has received significant attention in the recent machine learning literature due to the increasing need of producing accurate datasets in reasonable time Brendel et al. (2017); Papernot et al. (2017); Goodfellow et al. (2014); Antoniou et al. (2017); Volpi et al. (2018); Gao et al. (2019); Sinha et al. (2017); Gao et al. (2020); Magar et al. (2022); Zhou et al. (2020). In chemical and process systems engineering, prior research (i) starts from a small dataset, (ii) integrates new points into areas of interest and (iii) decides a set of stopping criteria Eason and Cremaschi (2014); Asghari et al. (2020); Garud et al. (2017).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Several authors propose sampling-based approaches to automatically augment the data-driven ANN with additional input/output pairs in a region of interest Eason and Cremaschi (2014); Alison Cozad (2014); Aroonsri Nuchitprasittichai (2012); Garud et al. (2017). Adding points to the dataset is important because the membrane behavior is represented by the dataset used for training rather than by mathematical equations Niu et al. (2022).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This paper develops a data augmentation algorithm, ADORO (Augmentated Dataset driven-by Optimization by Random Oscillation), that guides the dataset generation into all interesting regions found by our Multistart optimization algorithm in different scenarios. Prior work perturbs points close to a global solution, so our focus on Multistart is effectively an acknowledgment that, in process operation, there may be limited variation around a few data points Qin and Chiang (2019); Thebelt et al. (2022). In our case, only perturbing around the global solution leads to generating too few additional data points and only focusing on solutions that, after scrutiny, turn out to be non-robust. Considering different operating conditions improves both the quality and the robustness of the machine learning model.
texts.text: This paper develops a data augmentation algorithm, ADORO (Augmentated Dataset driven-by Optimization by Random Oscillation), that guides the dataset generation into all interesting regions found by our Multistart optimization algorithm in different scenarios. Prior work perturbs points close to a global solution, so our focus on Multistart is effectively an acknowledgment that, in process operation, there may be limited variation around a few data points Qin and Chiang (2019); Thebelt et al. (2022). In our case, only perturbing around the global solution leads to generating too few additional data points and only focusing on solutions that, after scrutiny, turn out to be non-robust. Considering different operating conditions improves both the quality and the robustness of the machine learning model.

texts.orig: We compare the new algorithm ADORO with more traditional adversarial augmented data (AAD) approaches from the machine learning literature Papernot et al. (2017); Volpi et al. (2018). These adversarial data augmentation techniques are mainly used in image recognition, natural language processing, and computer vision. But the AugLiChem Magar et al. (2022) library has been developed for chemical structures: AugLiChem perturbs crystals through random perturbation, or axis change, random rotation etc.

texts.orig: Our paper develops two data augmentation algorithms. First, the adversarial data augmentation algorithm (AAD) iteratively adds new points to our dataset by following the gradient descent of the loss. AAD adds points where our network struggles most to be accurate. Second, ADORO adds perturbed points (the neighbors) of the local optimal found through the Multistart optimization algorithm.
texts.text: Our paper develops two data augmentation algorithms. First, the adversarial data augmentation algorithm (AAD) iteratively adds new points to our dataset by following the gradient descent of the loss. AAD adds points where our network struggles most to be accurate. Second, ADORO adds perturbed points (the neighbors) of the local optimal found through the Multistart optimization algorithm.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Surrogate models have the advantage of reducing computational time in the optimization phase, as they lead to simplified models. Nevertheless, to be effectively used in an optimization strategy, a surrogate model needs two key elements: a high quality in terms of accuracy and a reasonable overall "creation" time. High accuracy is necessary for a large span of the variables to obtain a correct description of the optimization problem (feasible region and objective function shape). It is not possible to predict which search space points will be sampled by the optimization method (directly, or along the standard local search procedures), and a wrong evaluation of the constraints (and/or objective function) on such points can lead the optimization to the "wrong" search space portion, preventing the global optimization method to find the best local optimal solutions. The computational time needed to produce the surrogate model must be comparable (or smaller) than the time gained moving from an equation based model to the surrogate one.
texts.text: Surrogate models have the advantage of reducing computational time in the optimization phase, as they lead to simplified models. Nevertheless, to be effectively used in an optimization strategy, a surrogate model needs two key elements: a high quality in terms of accuracy and a reasonable overall "creation" time. High accuracy is necessary for a large span of the variables to obtain a correct description of the optimization problem (feasible region and objective function shape). It is not possible to predict which search space points will be sampled by the optimization method (directly, or along the standard local search procedures), and a wrong evaluation of the constraints (and/or objective function) on such points can lead the optimization to the "wrong" search space portion, preventing the global optimization method to find the best local optimal solutions. The computational time needed to produce the surrogate model must be comparable (or smaller) than the time gained moving from an equation based model to the surrogate one.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the previous section, we proved that an accurate surrogate model can be produced by means of an ANN and data obtained through simulation. The goal of the rest of the paper is to investigate different data augmentation techniques to maintain (or improve) the accuracy, reducing drastically the overall time needed to calibrate the ANN. Indeed, data augmentation techniques allow densifying the dataset only in some regions, thus obtaining high quality models with a reduced dataset. To assess the quality of the process and the produced models, we will focus on several quality indicators, as shown in Fig. 3.2.

texts.orig: We know that with 343K points we are able to achieve the target accuracy, but we want to keep the computational time low. For this reason, we aim to build a dataset of at most 125K points allowing us to reach a comparable model quality. To this aim, we have different possibilities: try datasets of different sizes up to 125K, or start with a smaller dataset, and use data augmentation techniques to improve the obtained model. In the latter case, we need to decide the size of the initial dataset and/or the number of iterations of data augmentation. By using Latin hypercube, we generate datasets of different sizes that we call data ini . Our idea is to exploit the quality of the current model, to add points in the area where the model is not good enough.
texts.text: We know that with 343K points we are able to achieve the target accuracy, but we want to keep the computational time low. For this reason, we aim to build a dataset of at most 125K points allowing us to reach a comparable model quality. To this aim, we have different possibilities: try datasets of different sizes up to 125K, or start with a smaller dataset, and use data augmentation techniques to improve the obtained model. In the latter case, we need to decide the size of the initial dataset and/or the number of iterations of data augmentation. By using Latin hypercube, we generate datasets of different sizes that we call data ini . Our idea is to exploit the quality of the current model, to add points in the area where the model is not good enough.

In what follows we describe the two techniques and report the results we obtained with this two procedures starting from different data ini and with a different size of the augmentation step.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The AAD procedure is an iterative one: the initial dataset is used to obtain a starting model, then the dataset is augmented by the new points (3.1), a new model is trained, and the augmentation procedure is repeated until some stopping condition is met. The AAD procedure doubles the number of points of the dataset at each iteration. We have two stopping criteria for our procedure: training error ≥ 1 . 2 e -6 or len(data ini ) ≤ 100 K . For all datasets, the procedure was stopped by the limit in size of the final dataset. By convention we refer to the final datasets as 10K+, 20K+ and 50K+. The results for the AAD procedure can be found in the Results Section 3.3.2, at the end of this chapter.
texts.text: The AAD procedure is an iterative one: the initial dataset is used to obtain a starting model, then the dataset is augmented by the new points (3.1), a new model is trained, and the augmentation procedure is repeated until some stopping condition is met. The AAD procedure doubles the number of points of the dataset at each iteration. We have two stopping criteria for our procedure: training error ≥ 1 . 2 e -6 or len(data ini ) ≤ 100 K . For all datasets, the procedure was stopped by the limit in size of the final dataset. By convention we refer to the final datasets as 10K+, 20K+ and 50K+. The results for the AAD procedure can be found in the Results Section 3.3.2, at the end of this chapter.

texts.text: More formally, our augmentation procedure works as follows. Let Q be the set of sample points obtained by the Multistart procedure. Let z q be the input variables of the surrogate model on these points, and u opt , and u sim denote the value of the output variables obtained respectively by the optimization and the simulation. For each of these points, we calculate the discrepancy between the optimization and the simulation:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Looking at the objective function allows us to evaluate also the cost of underestimating the purity, which could lead to unnecessarily complicated configurations or too expensive designs, which is what happens for the solution found by the model built by using AAD. In Fig. 3.11, we have selected the best solution in terms of the trade-off between feasibility and size of the dataset both for ADORO and for AAD. We plot both the normalized objective function value (histograms) and the effective purity (dots and connecting lines). It turns out that ADORO produces the best results since the cost is similar (and sometimes even better) to the one found with the 343K dataset. AAD pays the price of underestimating the purity, obtaining much higher objective function values.
texts.text: Looking at the objective function allows us to evaluate also the cost of underestimating the purity, which could lead to unnecessarily complicated configurations or too expensive designs, which is what happens for the solution found by the model built by using AAD. In Fig. 3.11, we have selected the best solution in terms of the trade-off between feasibility and size of the dataset both for ADORO and for AAD. We plot both the normalized objective function value (histograms) and the effective purity (dots and connecting lines). It turns out that ADORO produces the best results since the cost is similar (and sometimes even better) to the one found with the 343K dataset. AAD pays the price of underestimating the purity, obtaining much higher objective function values.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It is worth noticing, that the cost of the solution obtained using ADORO (40K) is smaller than the one obtained with the pure sampling strategy (see Fig. 3.11 c). We can explain this result, considering that ADORO refines the prediction model only around the interest points (local optima), and therefore that it needs fewer points than a pure sampling in order to obtain a model of equal or better quality.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In Table 3.7, we summarize the time needed for building and using a machine learning model instead of the original model based on the discretization of differential equations. Performing 1000 iterations of Multistart with the original model can take up to 10 hours when the purity required is high. Furthermore, the optimization suffers from high numerical instability leading to many unfeasible local searches. For all ANN models, the optimization time is significantly reduced: two up to 3 orders of magnitude. When we use an ML model built with ADORO, also the time needed to build the model is competitive. Indeed, if we use the ADORO model to study different combinations of purity and recovery the overall time of building the model and the optimization process is comparable to or even smaller than the one necessary for the model based on the discretization of the differential equation. Furthermore, the numerical issues are highly reduced, allowing the optimization strategy to better explore the search space.
