texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this last chapter, we show the case study for hydrogen production from a process engineering point of view. We report the results obtained for a binary system using two different types of membranes (H 2 selectivity and CO 2 selectivity), and for a ternary gas system. The process designs obtained with AAD are much more complex, using partial and self-recycling streams, which strongly decrease the robustness and operability of the process. So we decide to analyze the obtain results only for the 343K algorithm and ADORO.
texts.text: In this last chapter, we show the case study for hydrogen production from a process engineering point of view. We report the results obtained for a binary system using two different types of membranes (H 2 selectivity and CO 2 selectivity), and for a ternary gas system. The process designs obtained with AAD are much more complex, using partial and self-recycling streams, which strongly decrease the robustness and operability of the process. So we decide to analyze the obtain results only for the 343K algorithm and ADORO.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 4.1 present the CO 2 / H 2 trade-off. The CO 2 / H 2 separation with polymeric membrane offers remarkable and unconventional opportunities because of the very different behavior of membrane material. Contrary to the O 2 /N 2 case presented in the Introduction , some polymers present selectivities α CO 2 /H 2 greater or lower than 1 depending on the material, meaning that it will be possible to find H 2 or CO 2 selective materials. It brings a great opportunity for process design by adding a supplementary degree of freedom. Fig. 4.1 exhibits two boundary lines which represent the performance limit for CO 2 /H 2 separation as reported in Merkel et al. (2012a). As already explained in Section 1.2.2, the top line represents the limit for CO 2 selective membranes. CO 2 selective membranes are more used for processes which operate at room temperature and are based on polar, rubbery polymers, as reported in the following papers Lin and Freeman (2005); Lin et al. (2006); Car et al. (2008); Lau et al. (2011); Grainger and Hägg (2008); Zhao and Ho (2013); Lau et al. (2011). The lower line represents the limit for H 2 selective membranes. In this area the polymers are mostly glassy (or metallic or ceramic materials) Berchtold et al. (2012); Chung et al. (2006); Shao et al. (2009); Liu et al. (2012). As explained this is not a general behavior of membrane to propose reverse selectivity, many commercial polymers provide higher selectivity for H 2 than for other gases such as N 2 or CO 2 , as reported in Yuan et al. (2014b); Pathare and Agrawal (2010); Kookos (2002); Marriott and Sørensen (2003); Scholes et al. (2009); David et al. (2011). Membrane processes are, for most of them, pressure driven separation processes. The fastest components (highest permeabilities) are preferentially recovered on low pressure side (permeate) while the slowest ones are collected on the high pressure side (retentate), as shown in Sridhar et al. (2014).

texts.orig: The main expectation by mixing CO 2 and H 2 selective membranes in the same process is to achieve higher purity and recovery with lower costs. The targeted product is the H 2 , so from the membrane A (CO 2 selective) the retentate side will be the stream of interest while for the membrane B (H 2 selective) the permeate side will be targeted. In a two stage process membrane A should be able to first separate the hydrogen on the retentate and then refine the permeate (CO 2 + H 2 ) from the membrane A through the use of membrane B, Fig. 4.3.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The flow in input, FEED, is 1000 mol/s and the percentage of the binary system is equal to 0.4 for CO 2 and 0.6 for H 2 ; the percentage of the ternary gas is equal to 0.39 for CO 2 , 0.59 for H 2 and 0.02 for N 2 . We assume that all the membranes have the same pressure on the feed (and retentate) side (uniform P up ), and we assume that the pressure on the permeate side ( P down ) is greater or equal than 1 and lower than the pressure on the feed/retentate ( P down ≤ P up ). Therefore, whenever a permeated flow goes in input to a stage with higher pressure P up , the flow needs to go through a compressor. The input flow enters the membrane with a certain pressure equal to 50 bar. The pressure down, so the pressure on the permeate flow, can vary between 1 and 30 bar; instead, the pressure up between 50 and 100 bar. For the binary system, the pressure up was uniform (the same for all the membrane modules) while for the ternary we decide to use the pressure up not uniform. This decision makes the model more complicated. The global solution found with a ternary system AAB with uniform pressure up will be the same as the ternary system BAA's solution, due to the symmetries. Based on that we do not need to explore all the combinations between the different membrane materials. While if we use the not uniform pressure up all the combinations must be study, so symmetries can't be deleted.
texts.text: The flow in input, FEED, is 1000 mol/s and the percentage of the binary system is equal to 0.4 for CO 2 and 0.6 for H 2 ; the percentage of the ternary gas is equal to 0.39 for CO 2 , 0.59 for H 2 and 0.02 for N 2 . We assume that all the membranes have the same pressure on the feed (and retentate) side (uniform P up ), and we assume that the pressure on the permeate side ( P down ) is greater or equal than 1 and lower than the pressure on the feed/retentate ( P down ≤ P up ). Therefore, whenever a permeated flow goes in input to a stage with higher pressure P up , the flow needs to go through a compressor. The input flow enters the membrane with a certain pressure equal to 50 bar. The pressure down, so the pressure on the permeate flow, can vary between 1 and 30 bar; instead, the pressure up between 50 and 100 bar. For the binary system, the pressure up was uniform (the same for all the membrane modules) while for the ternary we decide to use the pressure up not uniform. This decision makes the model more complicated. The global solution found with a ternary system AAB with uniform pressure up will be the same as the ternary system BAA's solution, due to the symmetries. Based on that we do not need to explore all the combinations between the different membrane materials. While if we use the not uniform pressure up all the combinations must be study, so symmetries can't be deleted.

texts.orig: When we look at the o.f. we see that there is a significant discrepancy between the solution found by using the same type of membrane and the one with different membranes. We report as in Section 3.4 the normalized cost, by dividing the cost by 544 euros/ton reference, the cost of the objective function obtain with 343K for 90% purity and 99% recovery. For the highest combination of purity and recovery for the case-study AA the cost is at most 1.72 times the reference cost, while for the AB case-study it is at most 6.35 times the reference cost. This cost difference is in accordance with the AA and AB process architecture differences (Fig. 4.5, 4.6, 4.7, 4.8 and Fig. 4.9, 4.10, 4.11). In the AB case-study there are self-loop and a lot of split on the permeate side, which implies higher costs, due to the use of compressors. The larger is the flow passing through these components and the higher is the pressure swing, the higher is the o.f. costs.

Looking at the Table 4.4 the o.f. costs is at most 1.35 times the reference cost, so they are comparable to the 343K case for AA membranes. For both algorithms the pressure up is uniform, equal to 50 bar, while in the case of pressure down, ADORO increases it a little bit. For the area, ADORO finds solutions very close to the 343K case. For all combinations of purity and recovery the global optimization algorithm places a membrane with smaller area, between 300 and 700 m 2 , and a larger one, between 2500 and 4000 m 2 .

texts.orig: In this Section we show the results obtain by the sampling technique, already explained in the Chapter 2, for a ternary system. Our initial goal was to solve this case-study using ADORO algorithm, but because of a lack of time, only the preliminary results solving this case-study with an 80000-point dataset(80K) by using the Multistart algorithm will be reported. We augmented the degree of freedom of the problem, so try to solve it with such a small set of points is extremely challenging. For the sake of brevity, we report results not for all combinations of the two membrane types, but only for: AAA, AAB, BBA. And for 99% of purity and 90% of recovery.

texts.orig: In this thesis we studied the membrane gas separation design problem by means of an hybrid optimization and machine learning strategy.
texts.text: In this thesis we studied the membrane gas separation design problem by means of an hybrid optimization and machine learning strategy.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Start from the mathematical model and the optimization methods developed by Bozorg (2019), we were able to improve it and obtain a more accurate model. The design problem is represented as a non linear optimization problem. The methods proposed in Bozorg (2019) were used as a tool for effectively solving various application case studies. Nevertheless, the proposal approach has some limitations: small accuracy when high purities are requested and an important execution time for several case-studies. Indeed the equations representing the physic of the membrane are a discretization of a system of non-linear differential equations, and high number of them is necessary to obtain a good accuracy. For this reason, in this thesis, we have proposed a ML model to represent the behavior of each single membrane.
texts.text: Start from the mathematical model and the optimization methods developed by Bozorg (2019), we were able to improve it and obtain a more accurate model. Nevertheless, the proposal approach has some limitations: small accuracy when high purities are requested and an important execution time for several case-studies. Indeed the equations representing the physic of the membrane are a discretization of a system of non-linear differential equations, and high number of them is necessary to obtain a good accuracy. For this reason, in this thesis, we have proposed a ML model to represent the behavior of each single membrane.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The resulting model is then plugged in the original NLP model. Reducing the number of non-linear constraints by switching from discretization to the use of ANN, allows a faster optimization (at least two order of magnitude). To trade off modelization complexity with computational time, a single-hidden-layer ANN was chosen. The introduction of the ML allows to reduce the optimization time, nevertheless the time to obtain the model is important. We therefore decided to produce a lower quality model with a small dataset training and then improve it through change the training strategy, data augmentation. Our aim was to obtain a high quality model with a smaller computational time. We compared a classical data augmentation algorithm, which adds points following the gradient descent, with our new proposed strategy that we call ADORO.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: ADORO refines the prediction model only around the interest points (local optima). More precisely: we train the ANN model, perform a Multistart step, then add points only around local optima that are not well described by the ANN model.
texts.text: ADORO refines the prediction model only around the interest points (local optima). More precisely: we train the ANN model, perform a Multistart step, then add points only around local optima that are not well described by the ANN model.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It is already well-known that data-driven, machine learning models are vulnerable to inaccuracies outside the domain of their training data Schweidtmann et al. (2021). It is also well-known that adversarial data augmentation is a useful tool for increasing the trustworthiness of machine learning models. This work explores the possibility of augmenting the training data with points selected by Multistart optimization software. This data augmentation strategy implicitly uses the way optimization pushes decision variables to extremes to generate points where the neural network approximation and MEMSIC simulator disagree. One interesting item of note here is that global optimization over the entire decision-making problem to be of lesser interest: the new data augmentation strategy works best when we can generate many possible starting points, for instance using Multistart optimization.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A nonlinear programming problem (NLP) is a problem which includes at least one nonlinear function (objective function or the constrained). Several algorithms have been proposed to solve non linear optimization problems, such as Quasi-Newton Garcia and Mangasarian (1976), Genetic Algorithm Kramer (2017) etc.. When the problem contains both continuous and integer variables,the problem is called a Mixed Integer Non-Linear Programming(MINLP). And also for this type of problem exist algorithms for solving it such as Branch and Bound Clausen (1999), etc. So before choosing the optimization strategy the problem must be classified. As explained in Chapter 1 the membrane gas separation is classified as an MINLP, where the objective function and some of the constraints are not linear and the variables are both continuous and integer. Moreover, this problem is non-convex so finding the local optimum doesn't guarantee to have find the global one. This problem is a constrained problem and is not easy to solve. The global optimization program's aim is to minimize the objective function but also to define the feasible region, by solving the constrained problem.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: These assumptions are made to have only a continuous variable rather than a discrete one. With the hypothesis on the pressure we know where we need a compressor and vacuum pump before the optimization procedure. It also fixes the number of stages. In fact, with these assumptions, the membrane separation problem is not an MINLP but an NLP. This in any case will not be easy to solve given the high non-convexity and non-linearity of the problem, even though we have eliminated all discrete variables. By using only continuous variables the convergence time is greatly decreased. It is really important to have good starting points for the GO. We need to distinguish two types of variables, namely dependent ones (membrane areas, pressures, etc.) and independent ones (flow compositions, etc.); the respectively correspond to the design and the auxiliary variables. In this phase, we do not solve the complete problem but we solve a simplified problem without considering the constraints related to purity/recovery and the objective function. All independent variables are declared via the random generation, which is done by solving a series of linear equations. Once the independent variables have been
texts.text: These assumptions are made to have only a continuous variable rather than a discrete one. With the hypothesis on the pressure we know where we need a compressor and vacuum pump before the optimization procedure. It also fixes the number of stages. In fact, with these assumptions, the membrane separation problem is not an MINLP but an NLP. This in any case will not be easy to solve given the high non-convexity and non-linearity of the problem, even though we have eliminated all discrete variables. By using only continuous variables the convergence time is greatly decreased. It is really important to have good starting points for the GO. We need to distinguish two types of variables, namely dependent ones (membrane areas, pressures, etc.) and independent ones (flow compositions, etc.); the respectively correspond to the design and the auxiliary variables. In this phase, we do not solve the complete problem but we solve a simplified problem without considering the constraints related to purity/recovery and the objective function. All independent variables are declared via the random generation, which is done by solving a series of linear equations. Once the independent variables have been

texts.label: text
texts.orig: defined, the dependent variables are defined by solving the simplified model. For both models, the cell-related variables are initialized based on the simplifying assumptions.
texts.text: defined, the dependent variables are defined by solving the simplified model. For both models, the cell-related variables are initialized based on the simplifying assumptions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The GO strategy is a stochastic following the Multistart method or the Monotonic Basin Hopping (MBH) method. In the GO a local search on the random generating point is applied in a first stage. We choose KNITRO as a local solver, shown in Byrd et al. (2006). Once the local minimum is found, this point compared to the best value ever found by the algorithm Multistart, the algorithm is reported in Alg.1. The idea of combining global and local optimization characterizes some of the best stochastic GO algorithms.
texts.text: The GO strategy is a stochastic following the Multistart method or the Monotonic Basin Hopping (MBH) method. In the GO a local search on the random generating point is applied in a first stage. We choose KNITRO as a local solver, shown in Byrd et al. (2006). The idea of combining global and local optimization characterizes some of the best stochastic GO algorithms.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: On the other hand, the MBH is based on the fact that there likely is a better solution close to a local optimum, assuming that this local optimum is not the global one, Wales and Doye (1997); Locatelli and Schoen (2013). The MBH generates random a point, a local solver is applied on it and the first point of the procedure is defined. This point in the second step will be perturbed and locally optimized, if this solution is better than the previous one, the new local solution substitutes the old local solution. This procedure is applied until no improvements are found after a certain number of perturbations, as shown in Alg.2. The main difference between Multistart and MBH is that MBH doesn't restart each iteration from a new initial point but searches in the neighborhood of the local optimum considered.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The GO algorithm can start from different initial points of the search space. It can be considered as the mix of Multistart and MBH. It is a meta-heuristic. The Multistart and MBH algorithms respectively work on random generation and random perturbation to satisfy the constraints at the generated and perturbed points. This phase is really important because it defines the convergence speed of the algorithm. Starting from a good initial point means having a faster convergence and a higher probability to find a feasible solution. If the random perturbation and generation are not carefully done, the local search procedure could be unable to find a feasible point, or could a point very far from the original one, making the search "completely random" and therefore losing the capacity to search in the neighborhood typical of MBH Bozorg (2019). However, we have to consider the numerical instability so sometimes we could find some unfeasible solutions. Besides, due to the highly non-convex problem, some solutions might be found that are not real minimizers.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Machine Learning (ML) is a field of artificial intelligence (AI). The goal is to build a method that learns from data to improve performance on a certain set of assignments. The importance of the ML algorithm is because it can be applied in a wide variety of applications, including medicine, agriculture, computer video, biomedicine, etc., where conventional approaches perform poorly. These ML algorithms aim to use historical data as input to do the prediction of new output values. Depending on what we are trying to solve different techniques can be used. Tagged data refers to samples that have been tagged with one or more labels.
texts.text: Machine Learning (ML) is a field of artificial intelligence (AI). The goal is to build a method that learns from data to improve performance on a certain set of assignments. The importance of the ML algorithm is because it can be applied in a wide variety of applications, including medicine, agriculture, computer video, biomedicine, etc., where conventional approaches perform poorly. These ML algorithms aim to use historical data as input to do the prediction of new output values. Depending on what we are trying to solve different techniques can be used. Tagged data refers to samples that have been tagged with one or more labels.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · supervised learning : labeled data are used to train algorithms to classify data or predict outcomes accurately. The input data is fed into the model until the data is fitted appropriately. The performance of such algorithm is evaluated through some metrics, as the loss function until the error has been sufficiently minimized. This problem is divided into two types: (i) classification problem where test data has to be assigned to specific categories, and (ii) regression problems where a relationship between dependent and independent variables has to be found
texts.text: supervised learning : labeled data are used to train algorithms to classify data or predict outcomes accurately. The input data is fed into the model until the data is fitted appropriately. The performance of such algorithm is evaluated through some metrics, as the loss function until the error has been sufficiently minimized. This problem is divided into two types: (i) classification problem where test data has to be assigned to specific categories, and (ii) regression problems where a relationship between dependent and independent variables has to be found

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · unsupervised learning : an unlabeled dataset is used to train the algorithm. These algorithms are really useful to explore, i.e. to discover hidden correlations or data grouping without human intervention

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · semi-supervised learning : a small number of labeled examples and a number of unlabeled examples are used
texts.text: semi-supervised learning : a small number of labeled examples and a number of unlabeled examples are used

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · reinforcement learning : the model is trained by maximizing a reward in a particular situation. This technique is different from supervised learning because it doesn't need to label the input/output dataset. Reinforcement learning is a good balance between exploration and exploitation.

These neurons are connected to each other, by so-called synapses. In an analog manner, ANNs are made of nodes representing the neurons. texts.text: Artificial neural networks (ANN) are inspired by biological neural networks, the animal brains. ANN represent the human nervous system, composed by cells namely biological neurons. These neurons are connected to each other, by so-called synapses. In an analog manner, ANNs are made of nodes representing the neurons. These neurons receive a series of data inputs from other neurons and send these into other neurons again. After

texts.label: text
texts.orig: transforming them with a given function, they send the output of this function to down streaming neurons. Neurons of successive layers are connected via weights. By changing these weights appropriately the underlying model between input data and labels can be learned. The learning is done by changing the weights incrementally so that the prediction of the model is more and more accurate. The key to have a good ANN is mainly lies in the choice of the architecture used to represent the connection between the neurons. ANN can be simple single-layer perceptrons up to multilayer neural networks. Also we consider the number of neurons, Batch Size, optimizer, and etc. To find the best architecture a cross-validation is used. Different types of cross-validation exists:

Also we consider the number of neurons, Batch Size, optimizer, and etc. To find the best architecture a cross-validation is used. Different types of cross-validation exists:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We report the explanation for K-fold cross-validation. The dataset is split into K parts (the intersection will be empty), and for each iteration, a part of the dataset is used like a validation set and the rest of the dataset like a train set, this procedure is done cyclically. The training procedure is repeated K times with the same vector of hyperparameters on all the K couple of sets (training and validation sets) and this cycle is repeated as many times as the number of the vector of hyperparameters that must be tried. The goal of the cross-validation is to find the best combinations of hyperparameters, so the one that gives the lowest error on the validation set. There is two procedure that can be selected: grid search and random. In the grid search, all the possible combinations that we want of the output can be tried, but this procedure will ask a lot of time and could be useless for the evaluation. Because sometimes a good range of values is never tried. While with the random search, all the possible combinations on the range can be tried so the risk of wasting time for some evaluation is minimized. The idea is that when we find the best configuration if we change one hyperparameter the performance of the model could improve. In our case the size of the dataset is really huge so a random search for the K-fold Cross Validation procedure is better. So after the cross-validation we have to solve a nonconvex unconstrained problem. The algorithm used in this thesis is ADAM for smoother curves and it is a mixture between Batch Gradient Descent and SGD. In fact, the goal is to find a composite to overcome the limitations of both algorithms. We use a batch of fixed number of training examples, i.e. the mini-batch. In this case we fixed stepsize that allows to obtain optimal values of the error function weights. The gradient of the error function is updated with respect to the ANN weights. The calculation of the gradient proceeds from the deepest layer to the shallowest so we use the name 'backwards' and the gradient calculations of one layer are reused in the gradient calculation for the previous layer. Therefore, the gradient is efficiently calculated at each layer. This algorithm uses only a sample of the training dataset and this is where it gets its name 'mini-batch'. This leads to a lower process cost because the entire dataset is not used, but only a portion. This is extremely useful in cases, such as the one in question, where the training sample has a large size.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For more complicated problems a more complex network is needed. For that reason, Multi-layer Neural Networks are introduced. In this thesis, a Feed-Forward Neural Network (FFNN) is used, better known as Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP). This is a generalization of the single-layer perceptron. In this algorithm, the network is composed of one or more hidden layers, inserted between the input and output layers. The neurons are organized in different layers with forward connections between them. In Fig. B.2 a MLP is shown. So the input nodes connect the input variables x i = 1 , .., n to the network. Each layer l is made of neurons j = 1 ..J l with weights w l j and of biases b l j . The activation function of neuron j at the l -th layer is g l l . The output layer predicts y k , with k = 1 , ..., m where m is the number of output variables. Also from the MLP, a set of oriented weights can be extracted w l j,i that connect the input nodes or the outputs with each hidden layer I to the neurons j.
texts.text: For more complicated problems a more complex network is needed. For that reason, Multi-layer Neural Networks are introduced. In this thesis, a Feed-Forward Neural Network (FFNN) is used, better known as Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP). This is a generalization of the single-layer perceptron. In this algorithm, the network is composed of one or more hidden layers, inserted between the input and output layers. The neurons are organized in different layers with forward connections between them. In Fig. B.2 a MLP is shown. So the input nodes connect the input variables x i = 1 , .., n to the network. Each layer l is made of neurons j = 1 ..J l with weights w l j and of biases b l j . The activation function of neuron j at the l -th layer is g l l . The output layer predicts y k , with k = 1 , ..., m where m is the number of output variables. Also from the MLP, a set of oriented weights can be extracted w l j,i that connect the input nodes or the outputs with each hidden layer I to the neurons j.

table_cells.text: Representation of the input and output of the dimensionless simulator MEMSIC. As input takes the number of components n comp , the perme- ability for each components in the mixture Perm i , the input concentration of gas i x in i , the stage cut θ (percentage of permeate flow in output), and the pressure ratio φ ( P down /P up ). As output the dimensionless area st , the gas concentration x i of gas i in the retentate, and the concentration y i of gas i in the permeated. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .

The structure of ANN: it has three inputs: (i) φ pressure ratio (ii) x in i concentration of gas i (iii) θ stage cut and two outputs: (i) x i concentration of gas i in the retentate (ii) st dimensionless area. . . . . . . . . . . . . .

We report the dataset with respect to input variable with highlighted in dark red , the points where the simulator cannot converge. The recall that φ is the pressure ratio and θ the stage-cut. . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
Representation of the activation function: Sigmoid Function. . . . . . . .

grid.text: Representation of the input and output of the dimensionless simulator MEMSIC. As input takes the number of components n comp , the perme- ability for each components in the mixture Perm i , the input concentration of gas i x in i , the stage cut θ (percentage of permeate flow in output), and the pressure ratio φ ( P down /P up ). As output the dimensionless area st , the gas concentration x i of gas i in the retentate, and the concentration y i of gas i in the permeated. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .

The structure of ANN: it has three inputs: (i) φ pressure ratio (ii) x in i concentration of gas i (iii) θ stage cut and two outputs: (i) x i concentration of gas i in the retentate (ii) st dimensionless area. . . . . . . . . . . . . .

We report the dataset with respect to input variable with highlighted in dark red , the points where the simulator cannot converge. The recall that φ is the pressure ratio and θ the stage-cut. . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
Representation of the activation function: Sigmoid Function. . . . . . . .

table_cells.text: This table shows for each PURITY-RECOVERY combination number of locally optimal solutions found via Multistart. The more stringent the per- formance constraints are, the more difficult it is to find optimal solutions. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .

Features of the baseline (343K) and initial datasets data ini . We report the size for (i) the initial dataset;(ii) the final dataset, i.e. after the SVM- filtering procedure; the time in hours for (i) the generation of the dataset; (ii) for the cross-validation (iii) training of the best model found with the cross-validation . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .

We compare the best solution with its simulated counterpart in terms of system outputs. The models built with less than 50K points never reach the required purity, whereas the 50K dataset always produces a feasible solution but underestimates the purity when the performance requirement is 95% and 99%. On the other hand, the 125K only produces a feasible solution for purity equal to 99%. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .

The AAD data augmentation allows to reach the required purity when the starting size of the dataset is 50K apart from the 90% case. However, in general the purity is underestimated, resulting in solutions with purity higher than requested. In general, the AAD technique does not provide a better model than just sampling 50K points with a Latin hypercube strategy. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .

For each data ini , we report how many points are retained in the dataset by adding 5K, 10K, and 20K and filtering with the SVM for numerical instabilities. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .

The columns are in ascending order with respect to the size of the final dataset. It turns out that ADORO is able starting from 10K and 20K to produce a better model than the one with 50K points. Therefore ADORO succeeds in enforcing feasibility and high quality solution with a dataset of size lower than or equal to 50K. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .

Comparison between the computational time needed by the overall pro- cedure with and without an ML model. We consider ML models built with datasets of different sizes, including both the time for building the model and the (negligible) time for performing 1000 Multistart iterations. Then we add as a baseline the time needed to perform 1000 Multistart iterations when modeling the membrane behavior by the discretization of the differential equation . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
Stream condition for the two case-studies. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .

Objective function normalized cost, by dividing the o.f. cost by 544 eu- ros/ton, for the different designs, for each combination of purity and re- covery. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
